speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business were you in at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were commuting between Bergen-Belsen and Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in jewelry business, buying gold, buying dollars, and selling to the Russian army. That was black market, not legitimate. I want you should know that. I was in the black market business. I was very, very successful. I was a very, very good business man. I was-- I could approach anybody. I was not afraid from nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your life like at Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was giving a lot of people-- I give a lot of charity. When Israel was created, in 1948, I collected, one time, $50,000. In 1948, it was a lot of money. I personally, with another friend, collected $175,000 and $150,000. One was for the [NON-ENGLISH], and one was for the Haganah. I already collected money for them, because I was very proud that we were going to have a Jewish state. But that-- that was before the Jewish state. It was not proclaimed a Jewish state. After May, it was proclaimed a Jewish state. Came to us Landau once-- name-- by the name Landau. He got from me $50,000. I had to , [NON-ENGLISH] if you know that word, [NON-ENGLISH]. I had the guts to go to you, and to everyone, and to say, give me five-- I knew who has the money, and I said it's very urgent. We need that money to help ourselves to build our land. I had the guts, the courage, not like now I am. And I [NON-ENGLISH] out a lot of money. And I helped a lot of people, my friends. Not relatives, strangers. I don't know them. If anybody came into my house in Ber-- in Bergen-Belsen, before I went to Israel, they actually said, [INAUDIBLE] me. That means lend me some couple hundred dollars. It was an [INAUDIBLE]. We give it to him, and never paid me back. And I never want them back. I never wanted the money back, because it didn't mean nothing to me. In 19-- in 19-- when I was in Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a broker came into us. This was before they-- they devaluated the Deutche mark. And they offered that I should buy, in Hamburg, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a factory, like, in a small scale, like General Electric now, for 2 million mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had a whole room with mark. I could have buy it. And my brother wanted to buy. I said David, I will not do it. God bless you. See? If I'll buy this factory, I remain in Germany. I didn't want to remain in Germany. I hate it. Poland and Germany, I never wanted to go see back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, David, we will not going to buy this place, because if we were to buy it, we going to remain in Germany. We going to raise families in Germany, and I don't want to do that. I wanted to go to Israel. My-- my [NON-ENGLISH], my-- my destination was Israel. I loved Israel. I was a Zionist all my life. Not-- I wanted-- I couldn't be, because my parents didn't allow me to do it. But I, in my heart, was a Zionist all my life. I loved Israel. And I said, I will not buy this factory, because if we're going to buy it, we're going to remain there. And I didn't buy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you before, I had a lot of opportunities. I was a good looking kid. Money, I had plenty. And I am not-- I wasn't cheap with the money. I didn't know how money is-- is important to have. If I would know how money is good to have, I would be a very rich man. I made it and I spent it. I didn't hold onto the money which I have. Whoever came to me and wanted something, he got it from me. I was too good, and I'm not sorry for it. I'm not sorry. How I met my wife, as I told you, I had-- when I came to Bergen-Belsen, I had a friend-- friends which I was in concentration camp with them. And she wanted, I should marry her daughter. And I said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we know each other too good. Because she used to be in our barrack and I used to live in the barrack. She was a wonderful, beautiful, good looking girl, but she was not for me. She was not my type. And I said, our marriage will not survive. You know me, and you know your daughter. We are not good for each other. And this is the rea-- and I talked her out of it. I know you're going to feel bad, I said to her, but she-- we wouldn't be good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she said, you know what, Chaimo? She called me, Chaimo, like her son. She liked me very much. It was an older woman. And she said to me, you know what? I have a friend. She lives around here, and she's going to be good for you. I said, all right I'll see her. She-- she showed me to Paula.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went over. And she used to be-- she used to work for her brother. She had a little restaurant. Not like a restaurant, ice cream parlor. And I went over and buy myself an ice cream. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I went over, and I made a date with her. I forgot to tell you another story. When I was in Bergen-Belsen, me and my brother bought a ma-- two machine, two trucks, which to make blocks. And I went to a special school to learn how to make those blocks. I wanted to go to-- we wanted to li-- go to Israel. Then we have friends, we-- we-- we used to be in Bergen-Belsen. They emigrated to Israel before me. Then they wrote-- we were in contact with them and they wrote me, Chaim, I fell in. Don't come to Israel. If you have a chance, go to America. I spent $48,000 in 1947 [INAUDIBLE] I remember when they-- and I send away those-- the whole factory to Israel. I didn't go to Israel. I sold it for $12-- my share, I sold for $1,200.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Bergen-Belsen 1949-- 1949, December. We came to the United States in 1949, the 24 of December, I think. Either the 23rd-- 24, with General Blatchford, ship General Blatchford.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in the United States did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to New York. I actually-- I supposed to go to Detroit, Michigan. Not De-- not Detroit, Michigan state. And she had a brother in New-- in New York. Then we switched. Not we switched. We remained in New York. I-- I swi-- we came to New York. But from New York, I supposed to go to Detroit-- to Michigan state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then her brother said, you know what, Chaim? He wanted me-- he thought I'm going to be a partner with him. He knew how capable I am. He said, I want you here in New York. I didn't know who-- who I'm going to have in Detroit. I said, all right. What's the difference for me? This is strange and this is strange. I'm going to start in New York. When you look back upon your experiences, why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dumb luck. Not because how capable, not because I was smart. Only, only luck. If it would last a little bit longer, I wouldn't be here. I want that the world should know, and you worry the world should never forget, what we experienced. If we have to tell, I will-- I tell my children, my grandchildren, not to forget what we went through, not to forgive Germany, and the Pollack, what-- what-- what they did to my youth, and to my parents. I will never forgive them. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of times, dream a lot of times. I can't get over, many days, many nights, I'm living through the whole escaping. I told you the story. Believe me, it wasn't easy. [CRYING] My heart is bitter, bitter to tell the world what happened to our-- [INAUDIBLE] to our nation, not guilty, only because we were Jews. If we should tell our children to give them the best education, not to forget. I am saying to my children, to my daughters, give your children the best education, and not-- not to be ashamed that they're Jews. To be proud, not to be ashamed. [SOBBING] We'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you please introduce us to this very lovely young lady who's sitting next to you? This is my wife for the last 48 years. It's-- she's Polish, a very good wife, a good mother. Raised beautiful children, which I am very proud of them. Thanks to her, because I never had time to take care of my children, because I always went to work. She raised them. She gave-- she deserves the credit from-- for my beautiful two-- two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us something about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he didn't have time. It's true. He never saw the children, just Sunday morning. Went out in the morning, 5:00, and came back, they were sleeping. So I had to take care of everything myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes he used to say, I'm a [NON-ENGLISH], like an SS man.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very strict with the children, very strict.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very strict, because I--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I objected this. I said, Paula, don't raise children-- we-- we came from a school. We were-- We loved our parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a different-- I said, Paula, here is a different time. We have to adjust ourselves to this-- to this time and age. It's different now. She was very strict. But I don't blame her. She-- she did a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything special that you would like to add? Any kind of message that you would like to add at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say, special to our Jewish nation, to be united, not divided. We can only be successful if we going to be united. God forbid, if you're going to have a split in our nation, we're going to fall apart. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my wish now, because we-- it's a very critical time in Israel now. They killed our good men. [INAUDIBLE] I liked him very much. He was a man for peace. If you must have peace, we cannot-- the world cannot live in fighting. We have to-- we have to adjust ourselves. We have to live together with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name, Velvua Flancbaum, in-- in-- in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Viosk, Poland, 19-- 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1918. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Efroim Weinstein, [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Velvua Flancbaum, [INAUDIBLE] Matthews, and [INAUDIBLE], Poland, Viosk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1935. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Go?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1948, the 28th of January. On this day, I got married, and this is all of my-- my friends, which-- which attended the wedding. A few names of those people I remember, [INAUDIBLE], Norman [? Gitler ?], [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] [? Silverberg, ?] [? Guterman, ?] my brother, David Flancbaum, [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland, Viosk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my first child, Shirley. This is 1948, Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my lovely wife and myself, and my daughter, Shirley, and Florence. This was taken in 19-- 1962 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in South [? Holbrook. ?] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is me and my wife, my brother, and my sister-in-law. This picture was taken in New York, in 1975. This is good. This was taken in 1985, in my house. Here is a man, which I survived with in a concentration camp. His name is Efroim Weinstein, and his wife. He lives now in Argentina. And this is my lovely wife, Paula Flancbaum, Shirley Flancbaum, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Florence, my son-in-law, Barry, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and all my grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my four-- my-- my beautiful grandchildren, Joshua, Julie, Melissa, Mark. This was taken in New York, 1991. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Shirley Hackel now, and my son-in-law Henry Hackel. It was taken in New York, in 1985. This is my sweet daughter, the baby, my baby, Florence, and her wonderful husband, Barry Sideroff. This picture was taken 1991, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] New York.
